title: Comparing HC Estimators
text: Comparisons start one fitted model. use OLS fit methods.
code: schools <- PublicSchools |>   dplyr::mutate(     income_scaled = income / 10000,     income_scaled_sq = income_scaled^2   )  fit <- lm(expenditure ~ income_scaled + income_scaled_sq, data = schools)

title: Comparing HC Estimators
text: type argument selects HC estimator. Store result named list later extraction straightforward.
code: methods <- c("hc0", "hc3", "hc4", "hc4m", "hcbeta")  results <- purrr::map(methods, \(method) {   hcinfer(fit, type = method) }) names(results) <- methods

title: Comparing HC Estimators
text: applied comparisons focus one coefficients. helper uses tests() confint() extract one coefficient adds method name. Add interval widths goal compare conservative estimators coefficient.
code: extract_term <- function(result, method, term = "income_scaled_sq") {   row <- tests(result, parm = term)   ci <- confint(result, parm = term)    tibble::tibble(     method = method,     estimate = row$estimate,     std_error = row$std_error,     p_value = row$p_value,     conf_low = ci$conf_low,     conf_high = ci$conf_high,     reject = row$reject   ) }  comparison <- purrr::imap(results, extract_term) comparison <- dplyr::bind_rows(comparison) comparison #> # A tibble: 5 × 7 #>   method estimate std_error p_value conf_low conf_high reject #>   <chr>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <lgl>  #> 1 hc0       1587.      830.  0.0559    -39.7     3214. FALSE  #> 2 hc3       1587.     1995.  0.426   -2324.      5498. FALSE  #> 3 hc4       1587.     5489.  0.772   -9171.     12345. FALSE  #> 4 hc4m      1587.     2553.  0.534   -3417.      6591. FALSE  #> 5 hcbeta    1587.     1547.  0.305   -1446.      4620. FALSE comparison <- comparison |>   dplyr::mutate(interval_width = conf_high - conf_low)  comparison #> # A tibble: 5 × 8 #>   method estimate std_error p_value conf_low conf_high reject interval_width #>   <chr>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <lgl>           <dbl> #> 1 hc0       1587.      830.  0.0559    -39.7     3214. FALSE           3254. #> 2 hc3       1587.     1995.  0.426   -2324.      5498. FALSE           7821. #> 3 hc4       1587.     5489.  0.772   -9171.     12345. FALSE          21516. #> 4 hc4m      1587.     2553.  0.534   -3417.      6591. FALSE          10009. #> 5 hcbeta    1587.     1547.  0.305   -1446.      4620. FALSE           6066.

title: Comparing HC Estimators
text: simple plot can help show estimators differ selected coefficient.
code: ggplot2::ggplot(comparison, ggplot2::aes(x = method, y = std_error)) +   ggplot2::geom_col(fill = "#305c8a") +   ggplot2::labs(     x = "Estimator",     y = "Robust standard error for the quadratic income term"   ) +   ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

text: confint() returns tibble one result. Use stored comparison table want intervals several methods side side.

title: Comparing HC Estimators
text: Use vcov_hc() need covariance matrix diagnostics, coefficient tests. Plot adjustment factors leverage values covariance object.  covariance object also summary method.
code: cov_hc3 <- vcov_hc(fit, type = "hc3") cov_hc3 #>  #> ── 🥪 HC3 robust covariance ──────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> 📐 Model: `expenditure ~ income_scaled + income_scaled_sq` #> Dimension: 3 x 3 #> Observations: 50 #> Parameters: 3 #> 🎯 Maximum leverage: 0.6508 #> ⚖️ Maximum robust weight: 8.2009 #> Use `vcov()` to extract the stored covariance matrix. vcov(cov_hc3) #>                  (Intercept) income_scaled income_scaled_sq #> (Intercept)          1199026      -3256564          2180884 #> income_scaled       -3256564       8853073         -5934046 #> income_scaled_sq     2180884      -5934046          3980990 plot(cov_hc3) summary(cov_hc3) #>  #> ── 🥪 HC3 robust covariance summary ──────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── 📐 Model ── #>  #> Formula: `expenditure ~ income_scaled + income_scaled_sq` #> Observations: 50 | Parameters: 3 | Residual df: 47 #>  #> ── 🎯 Leverage diagnostics ── #>  #> # A tibble: 6 × 2 #>   statistic value   #>   <chr>     <chr>   #> 1 minimum   0.02669 #> 2 q1        0.03106 #> 3 median    0.03912 #> 4 mean      0.06    #> 5 q3        0.04962 #> 6 maximum   0.6508 #> Maximum leverage: observation 2 (index 2), value 0.6508 #> Average leverage: 0.0600 #> Concentration: 10.85 x average leverage #>  #> ── ⚖️ Robust weights ── #>  #> # A tibble: 6 × 2 #>   statistic value #>   <chr>     <chr> #> 1 minimum   1.056 #> 2 q1        1.065 #> 3 median    1.083 #> 4 mean      1.251 #> 5 q3        1.107 #> 6 maximum   8.201 #> Maximum weight: observation 2 (index 2), value 8.2009 #> Median weight: 1.0831 #> Concentration: 7.57 x median weight #>  #> ── ⚙️ Method parameters ── #>  #> No additional method parameters.

title: Comparing HC Estimators
text: covariance inference objects store robust weights leverage values. leverage values depend fitted model, weights depend HC estimator. next figure mirrors empirical display HCbeta paper: adjustment factors plotted leverages HC3, HC4, HC4m, HCbeta.
code: covariances <- purrr::map(methods, \(method) {   vcov_hc(fit, type = method) }) names(covariances) <- methods  diagnostic_comparison <- purrr::imap(covariances, \(cov, method) {   tibble::tibble(     method = method,     max_leverage = max(cov$leverage),     max_weight = max(cov$weights),     median_weight = stats::median(cov$weights)   ) }) diagnostic_comparison <- dplyr::bind_rows(diagnostic_comparison) diagnostic_comparison #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>   method max_leverage max_weight median_weight #>   <chr>         <dbl>      <dbl>         <dbl> #> 1 hc0           0.651       1             1    #> 2 hc3           0.651       8.20          1.08 #> 3 hc4           0.651      67.3           1.03 #> 4 hc4m          0.651      13.9           1.05 #> 5 hcbeta        0.651       4.58          1.19 figure_methods <- c("hc3", "hc4", "hc4m", "hcbeta") figure_covariances <- purrr::map(figure_methods, \(method) {   vcov_hc(fit, type = method) }) names(figure_covariances) <- figure_methods  weight_comparison <- purrr::imap(figure_covariances, \(cov, method) {   tibble::tibble(     method = cov$label,     leverage = cov$leverage,     weight = cov$weights,     high_leverage = cov$leverage > 3 * cov$p / cov$n   ) }) |>   dplyr::bind_rows() |>   dplyr::mutate(     method = factor(method, levels = c("HC3", "HC4", "HC4m", "HCbeta"))   )  ggplot2::ggplot(weight_comparison, ggplot2::aes(x = leverage, y = weight)) +   ggplot2::geom_point(     ggplot2::aes(color = high_leverage),     size = 1.8,     alpha = 0.85   ) +   ggplot2::facet_wrap(~method, scales = "free_y", ncol = 2) +   ggplot2::scale_color_manual(     values = c(`FALSE` = "#2c5f8a", `TRUE` = "#c0392b"),     guide = "none"   ) +   ggplot2::labs(     x = expression(h[t]),     y = expression(g[t])   ) +   ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +   ggplot2::theme(     strip.text = ggplot2::element_text(face = "bold"),     panel.grid.minor = ggplot2::element_blank()   )

title: Using HCbeta
text: Fit OLS model request HCbeta explicitly. equivalent default hcinfer(fit) call.
code: library(hcinfer)  schools <- PublicSchools |>   dplyr::mutate(     income_scaled = income / 10000,     income_scaled_sq = income_scaled^2   )  fit <- lm(expenditure ~ income_scaled + income_scaled_sq, data = schools) result <- hcinfer(fit, type = "hcbeta")  summary(result) #>  #> ── 🔎 HCbeta robust inference summary ────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── 📐 Model ── #>  #> Formula: `expenditure ~ income_scaled + income_scaled_sq` #> Observations: 50 | Parameters: 3 | Residual df: 47 #>  #> ── 🥪 Robust covariance ── #>  #> Estimator: HCbeta #> Confidence level: 95.0% | Normal critical value: 1.9600 #> Tests are two-sided normal Wald tests, one coefficient at a time. #> Test results use alpha = 0.050. #>  #> ── 🎯 Leverage diagnostics ── #>  #> # A tibble: 6 × 2 #>   statistic value   #>   <chr>     <chr>   #> 1 minimum   0.02669 #> 2 q1        0.03106 #> 3 median    0.03912 #> 4 mean      0.06    #> 5 q3        0.04962 #> 6 maximum   0.6508 #> Maximum leverage: observation 2 (index 2), value 0.6508 #> Average leverage: 0.0600 #> Concentration: 10.85 x average leverage #>  #> ── ⚖️ Robust weights ── #>  #> # title: Using HCbeta
text: Use tests() extract coefficient-level Wald results tibble. example, extract robust standard error p-value quadratic income term.
code: tests(result) #> # A tibble: 3 × 8 #>   term             estimate null_value std_error z_value p_value alpha reject #>   <chr>               <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> <lgl>  #> 1 (Intercept)          833.          0      851.   0.979   0.328  0.05 FALSE  #> 2 income_scaled      -1834.          0     2309.  -0.794   0.427  0.05 FALSE  #> 3 income_scaled_sq    1587.          0     1547.   1.03    0.305  0.05 FALSE dplyr::select(tests(result, parm = "income_scaled_sq"), term, std_error, p_value) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   term             std_error p_value #>   <chr>                <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1 income_scaled_sq     1547.   0.305

title: Using HCbeta
text: HCbeta stores user-facing constants estimated quantities method_params. useful entries routine inspection : values diagnostics. routine use, defaults usually left unchanged.

title: Using HCbeta
text: HCbeta, like estimators, stores leverage values robust weights. table shows observations largest leverages. can also sort robust weight. covariance object can plotted directly display adjustment factors leverages.
code: diagnostics <- tibble::tibble(   observation = result$observation,   leverage = result$leverage,   weight = result$weights,   residual = result$residuals ) |>   dplyr::mutate(     state = schools$state[as.integer(observation)],     .before = leverage   )  diagnostics |>   dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(leverage)) |>   dplyr::slice_head(n = 5) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   observation state          leverage weight residual #>   <chr>       <chr>             <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 2           Alaska           0.651    4.58   110.   #> 2 48          Washington DC    0.208    1.61  -161.   #> 3 24          Mississippi      0.200    1.59   -44.0  #> 4 4           Arkansas         0.0887   1.30   -30.5  #> 5 40          South Carolina   0.0794   1.28     8.64 diagnostics |>   dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(weight)) |>   dplyr::slice_head(n = 5) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   observation state          leverage weight residual #>   <chr>       <chr>             <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 2           Alaska           0.651    4.58   110.   #> 2 48          Washington DC    0.208    1.61  -161.   #> 3 24          Mississippi      0.200    1.59   -44.0  #> 4 4           Arkansas         0.0887   1.30   -30.5  #> 5 40          South Carolina   0.0794   1.28     8.64 plot(vcov_hc(fit, type = "hcbeta"))

title: Using HCbeta
text: HCbeta constants can passed .... sensitivity check compares default result small set alternative settings. table helps identify whether reported inference sensitive small changes HCbeta tuning constants. defaults remain recommended starting point.
code: sensitivity_results <- list(   default = hcinfer(fit, type = "hcbeta"),   c1_5 = hcinfer(fit, type = "hcbeta", c1 = 5),   tighter_truncation = hcinfer(fit, type = "hcbeta", lower = 0.02, upper = 0.98) )  sensitivity <- purrr::imap(sensitivity_results, \(res, setting) {   row <- tests(res, parm = "income_scaled_sq")   ci <- confint(res, parm = "income_scaled_sq")    tibble::tibble(     setting = setting,     std_error = row$std_error,     p_value = row$p_value,     conf_low = ci$conf_low,     conf_high = ci$conf_high,     max_weight = max(res$weights)   ) }) sensitivity <- dplyr::bind_rows(sensitivity) sensitivity #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   setting            std_error p_value conf_low conf_high max_weight #>   <chr>                  <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 default                1547.   0.305   -1446.     4620.       4.58 #> 2 c1_5                   1292.   0.219    -945.     4119.       3.02 #> 3 tighter_truncation     1547.   0.305   -1446.     4620.       4.58

what: Compare HCbeta with one classical estimator
title: Using HCbeta
text: quick comparison, put HCbeta next classical estimator HC3.
code: hc3 <- hcinfer(fit, type = "hc3")  extract_income <- function(res, method) {   row <- tests(res, parm = "income_scaled_sq")   ci <- confint(res, parm = "income_scaled_sq")   tibble::tibble(     method = method,     estimate = row$estimate,     std_error = row$std_error,     p_value = row$p_value,     conf_low = ci$conf_low,     conf_high = ci$conf_high   ) }  compare_hc3 <- dplyr::bind_rows(   extract_income(result, "hcbeta"),   extract_income(hc3, "hc3") ) compare_hc3 #> # A tibble: 2 × 6 #>   method estimate std_error p_value conf_low conf_high #>   <chr>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 hcbeta    1587.     1547.   0.305   -1446.     4620. #> 2 hc3       1587.     1995.   0.426   -2324.     5498.

title: Using HCbeta
text: routine analyses: Fit OLS model lm(). Run hcinfer(fit) hcinfer(fit, type = "hcbeta"). Use summary(result) readable output. Use tests(result), confint(result), coef(result), vcov(result) extracted values. Inspect result$weights, result$leverage, result$method_params diagnostics needed. Use vignette("hcinfer-comparison") want compare HCbeta estimators implemented package.

title: Introduction to hcinfer
text: examples use PublicSchools data included package. model quadratic income model used HCbeta application. call lm() omits observations missing model variables.
code: library(hcinfer)  schools <- PublicSchools schools$income_scaled <- schools$income / 10000 schools$income_scaled_sq <- schools$income_scaled^2  fit <- lm(expenditure ~ income_scaled + income_scaled_sq, data = schools) fit #>  #> Call: #> lm(formula = expenditure ~ income_scaled + income_scaled_sq,  #>     data = schools) #>  #> Coefficients: #>      (Intercept)     income_scaled  income_scaled_sq   #>            832.9           -1834.2            1587.0

title: Introduction to hcinfer
text: Use hc_methods() list estimators default arguments. available types "hc0", "hc1", "hc2", "hc3", "hc4", "hc4m", "hc5", "hc5m", "hcbeta".
code: hc_methods() #> # A tibble: 9 × 4 #>   type   label  description                                    default_arguments #>   <chr>  <chr>  <chr>                                          <chr>             #> 1 hc0    HC0    White heteroskedasticity-consistent estimator. none              #> 2 hc1    HC1    HC0 with degrees-of-freedom scaling.           none              #> 3 hc2    HC2    Leverage-adjusted estimator with exponent 1.   none              #> 4 hc3    HC3    Leverage-adjusted estimator with exponent 2.   none              #> 5 hc4    HC4    Adaptive leverage correction by Cribari-Neto.  none              #> 6 hc4m   HC4m   Modified HC4 correction by Cribari-Neto and d… none              #> 7 hc5    HC5    High-leverage correction by Cribari-Neto, Sou… k = 0.7           #> 8 hc5m   HC5m   Modified HC5 correction by Li, Zhang, Zhang, … k = 0.7, k1 = 1,… #> 9 hcbeta HCbeta Beta-distribution leverage correction.         c1 = 7, c2 = 0.7…

title: Introduction to hcinfer
text: Use hcinfer() compute robust inference lm() object. default estimator HCbeta. Use summary() readable report coefficient tests, confidence intervals, leverage diagnostics, robust weight diagnostics. can choose another estimator type.
code: result <- hcinfer(fit) result #>  #> ── HCbeta robust inference ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Model: `expenditure ~ income_scaled + income_scaled_sq` #> Observations: 50 | Parameters: 3 #> Robust covariance: HCbeta #> Confidence level: 95.0% | Normal critical value: 1.9600 #> Use `summary()` for p-values, test results, confidence intervals, and #> diagnostics. summary(result) #>  #> ── HCbeta robust inference summary ───────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Model ── #>  #> Formula: `expenditure ~ income_scaled + income_scaled_sq` #> Observations: 50 | Parameters: 3 | Residual df: 47 #>  #> ── Robust covariance ── #>  #> Estimator: HCbeta #> Confidence level: 95.0% | Normal critical value: 1.9600 #> Tests are two-sided normal Wald tests, one coefficient at a time. #> Test results use alpha = 0.050. #>  #> ── Leverage diagnostics ── #>  #> # A tibble: 6 × 2 #>   statistic value   #>   <chr>     <chr>   #> 1 minimum   0.02669 #> 2 q1        0.03106 #> 3 median    0.03912 #> 4 mean      0.06    #> 5 q3        0.04962 #> 6 maximum   0.6508 #> Maximum leverage: observation 2 (index 2), value 0.6508 #> Average leverage: 0.0600 #> Concentration: 10.85 x average leverage #>  #> ── Robust weights ── #>  #> # A tibble: 6 × 2 #>   statistic value #>   <chr>     <chr> #> 1 minimum   1.156 #> 2 q1        1.167 #> 3 median    1.187 #> 4 mean      1.276 #> 5 q3        1.212 #> 6 maximum   4.581 #> Maximum weight: observation 2 (index 2), value 4.5807 #> Median weight: 1.1869 #> Concentration: 3.86 x median weight #>  #> ── Method parameters ── #>  #> # A tibble: 12 × 3 #>    parameter value    role               #>    <chr>     <chr>    <chr>              #>  1 c1        7        method constant    #>  2 c2        0.75     method constant    #>  3 lower     0.01     method constant    #>  4 upper     0.99     method constant    #>  5 mu_hat    0.94     estimated quantity #>  6 s2_w      0.008504 estimated quantity #>  7 phi_hat   5.632    estimated quantity #>  8 a_hat     5.294    estimated quantity #>  9 b_hat     0.3379   estimated quantity #> 10 zeta      0.5      estimated quantity #> 11 a_tilde   3.147    estimated quantity #> 12 b_tilde   0.669    estimated quantity #>  #> ── Coefficient tests ── #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 9 #>   term             estimate robust_se z       p_value alpha test_result      #>   <chr>            <chr>    <chr>     <chr>   <chr>   <chr> <chr>            #> 1 (Intercept)      832.9    850.7     0.9791  0.328   0.050 do not reject H0 #> 2 income_scaled    -1834    2309      -0.7945 0.427   0.050 do not reject H0 #> 3 income_scaled_sq 1587     1547      1.026   0.305   0.050 do not reject H0 #>   ci             ci_relation   #>   <chr>          <chr>         #> 1 [-834.3, 2500] includes null #> 2 [-6359, 2691]  includes null #> 3 [-1446, 4620]  includes null #>  #> ── Confidence intervals ── #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 4 #>   term             null_value interval       interpretation #>   <chr>            <chr>      <chr>          <chr>          #> 1 (Intercept)      0          [-834.3, 2500] includes null  #> 2 income_scaled    0          [-6359, 2691]  includes null  #> 3 income_scaled_sq 0          [-1446, 4620]  includes null #> test_result is based on p_value < alpha. Do not reject H0 does not mean that H0 #> is true. result_hc3 <- hcinfer(fit, type = "hc3") result_hc3 #>  #> ── HC3 robust inference ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Model: `expenditure ~ income_scaled + income_scaled_sq` #> Observations: 50 | Parameters: 3 #> Robust covariance: HC3 #> Confidence level: 95.0% | Normal critical value: 1.9600 #> Use `summary()` for p-values, test results, confidence intervals, and #> diagnostics.

title: Introduction to hcinfer
text: Use tests() extract coefficient-level Wald tests tibble. Select one coefficient name position. Change alpha update rejection decision. test statistic p-value recomputed.
code: tests(result) #> # A tibble: 3 × 8 #>   term             estimate null_value std_error z_value p_value alpha reject #>   <chr>               <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> <lgl>  #> 1 (Intercept)          833.          0      851.   0.979   0.328  0.05 FALSE  #> 2 income_scaled      -1834.          0     2309.  -0.794   0.427  0.05 FALSE  #> 3 income_scaled_sq    1587.          0     1547.   1.03    0.305  0.05 FALSE tests(result, parm = "income_scaled_sq") #> # A tibble: 1 × 8 #>   term             estimate null_value std_error z_value p_value alpha reject #>   <chr>               <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> <lgl>  #> 1 income_scaled_sq    1587.          0     1547.    1.03   0.305  0.05 FALSE tests(result, parm = 3) #> # A tibble: 1 × 8 #>   term             estimate null_value std_error z_value p_value alpha reject #>   <chr>               <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> <lgl>  #> 1 income_scaled_sq    1587.          0     1547.    1.03   0.305  0.05 FALSE tests(result, alpha = 0.10) #> # A tibble: 3 × 8 #>   term             estimate null_value std_error z_value p_value alpha reject #>   <chr>               <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> <lgl>  #> 1 (Intercept)          833.          0      851.   0.979   0.328   0.1 FALSE  #> 2 income_scaled      -1834.          0     2309.  -0.794   0.427   0.1 FALSE  #> 3 income_scaled_sq    1587.          0     1547.   1.03    0.305   0.1 FALSE

title: Introduction to hcinfer
text: Use confint() extract robust confidence intervals. can select coefficient change confidence level.
code: confint(result) #> # A tibble: 3 × 4 #>   term             conf_low conf_high level #>   <chr>               <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 (Intercept)         -834.     2500.  0.95 #> 2 income_scaled      -6359.     2691.  0.95 #> 3 income_scaled_sq   -1446.     4620.  0.95 confint(result, parm = "income_scaled_sq") #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   term             conf_low conf_high level #>   <chr>               <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 income_scaled_sq   -1446.     4620.  0.95 confint(result, parm = "income_scaled_sq", level = 0.90) #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   term             conf_low conf_high level #>   <chr>               <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 income_scaled_sq    -958.     4132.   0.9

title: Introduction to hcinfer
text: coef() method returns OLS estimates stored result. vcov() method extracts robust covariance matrix stored hcinfer object. Robust standard errors square roots diagonal entries.
code: coef(result) #>      (Intercept)    income_scaled income_scaled_sq  #>         832.9144       -1834.2029        1587.0423 robust_vcov <- vcov(result) robust_vcov #>                  (Intercept) income_scaled income_scaled_sq #> (Intercept)         723617.6      -1962262          1312195 #> income_scaled     -1962262.2       5329884         -3569755 #> income_scaled_sq   1312195.3      -3569755          2394627 sqrt(diag(robust_vcov)) #>      (Intercept)    income_scaled income_scaled_sq  #>         850.6572        2308.6541        1547.4583

title: Introduction to hcinfer
text: Use plot() hcinfer object display robust confidence intervals.  Select one coefficient parm.  Use plot() vcov_hc() object show leverage values HC adjustment factors.  Set label_top control many observations largest adjustment factors labeled.
code: plot(result) plot(result, parm = "income_scaled_sq") plot(cov_hcbeta) plot(vcov_hc(fit, type = "hc3"), label_top = 2)

title: Introduction to hcinfer
text: following base R code compares HCbeta HC3 quadratic income coefficient.
code: test_hcbeta <- tests(result, parm = "income_scaled_sq") test_hc3 <- tests(result_hc3, parm = "income_scaled_sq")  comparison <- data.frame(   estimator = c("HCbeta", "HC3"),   estimate = c(test_hcbeta$estimate, test_hc3$estimate),   robust_se = c(test_hcbeta$std_error, test_hc3$std_error),   p_value = c(test_hcbeta$p_value, test_hc3$p_value) )  comparison #>   estimator estimate robust_se   p_value #> 1    HCbeta 1587.042  1547.458 0.3050896 #> 2       HC3 1587.042  1995.242 0.4263730

title: Introduction to hcinfer
text: routine use, workflow short.
code: fit <- lm(y ~ x1 + x2, data = data) result <- hcinfer(fit, type = "hcbeta")  summary(result) tests(result) confint(result) plot(result)

title: Heteroskedasticity-consistent inference for linear models
text: default estimator HCbeta. Use tests() confint() extract main inferential quantities tibbles.
code: library(hcinfer)  schools <- PublicSchools schools$income_scaled <- schools$income / 10000 schools$income_scaled_sq <- schools$income_scaled^2  fit <- lm(expenditure ~ income_scaled + income_scaled_sq, data = schools)  result <- hcinfer(fit) tests(result) #> # A tibble: 3 × 8 #>   term             estimate null_value std_error z_value p_value alpha reject #>   <chr>               <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> <lgl>  #> 1 (Intercept)          833.          0      851.   0.979   0.328  0.05 FALSE  #> 2 income_scaled      -1834.          0     2309.  -0.794   0.427  0.05 FALSE  #> 3 income_scaled_sq    1587.          0     1547.   1.03    0.305  0.05 FALSE confint(result) #> # A tibble: 3 × 4 #>   term             conf_low conf_high level #>   <chr>               <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 (Intercept)         -834.     2500.  0.95 #> 2 income_scaled      -6359.     2691.  0.95 #> 3 income_scaled_sq   -1446.     4620.  0.95

title: Heteroskedasticity-consistent Wald inference — hcinfer
text: object ordinary least squares model fitted stats::lm(). type character string specifying HC estimator. default "hcbeta". alpha Significance level. confidence level 1 - alpha. null Null values coefficient tests. Use scalar test coefficients value, numeric vector one value per coefficient. ... Method-specific constants passed vcov_hc(). Defaults documented vcov_hc() can inspected hc_methods().

title: Heteroskedasticity-consistent Wald inference — hcinfer
text: object class hcinfer containing fitted HC covariance estimator, coefficient tests, p-values, confidence intervals, diagnostics, method parameters.

title: Heteroskedasticity-consistent Wald inference — hcinfer
text: coefficient, hcinfer tests $$H_0: \beta_j = \beta_j^{(0)}$$ two-sided alternative using statistic $$z_j = \frac{\hat\beta_j - \beta_j^{(0)}} {\sqrt{[\widehat{\Psi}_{HC}]_{jj}}}.$$ reference distribution standard normal distribution. Confidence intervals Wald intervals obtained direct inversion test, $$\hat\beta_j \pm z_{1 - \alpha / 2} \sqrt{[\widehat{\Psi}_{HC}]_{jj}}.$$ Bootstrap intervals Student t quantiles used.

title: Extract coefficient test results — tests
text: coefficient, stored test $$H_0: \beta_j = \beta_j^{(0)}$$ two-sided alternative. test statistic $$z_j = \frac{\hat\beta_j - \beta_j^{(0)}} {\sqrt{[\widehat{\Psi}_{HC}]_{jj}}},$$ p-value \(2\,\Phi(-|z_j|)\), \(\Phi\) standard normal distribution function. null value \(\beta_j^{(0)}\) one stored object, set hcinfer() called. test different null value, rerun hcinfer() desired null argument.

title: Heteroskedasticity-consistent covariance estimator — vcov_hc
text: object ordinary least squares model fitted stats::lm(). type character string specifying HC estimator. default "hcbeta". ... Method-specific constants. Unknown names rejected. See Details accepted names, defaults, parameter domains.

title: Heteroskedasticity-consistent covariance estimator — vcov_hc
text: object class hcinfer_vcov. covariance matrix stored object$vcov returned directly vcov().

title: Heteroskedasticity-consistent covariance estimator — vcov_hc
text: linear model design matrix \(X\), OLS residuals \(\hat e_t\), HC weights \(g_t\), estimator $$\widehat{\Psi}_{HC} = (X'X)^{-1} X' \widehat{\Omega} X (X'X)^{-1},$$ \(\widehat{\Omega} = diag(\hat e_t^2 g_t)\). supported estimators "hc0", "hc1", "hc2", "hc3", "hc4", "hc4m", "hc5", "hc5m", "hcbeta". Additional arguments ... method-specific. defaults : "hc0", "hc1", "hc2", "hc3", "hc4", "hc4m": method-specific arguments. "hc5": k = 0.7. "hc5m": k = 0.7, k1 = 1, k2 = 0, k3 = 1, gamma1 = 1, gamma2 = 1.5. "hcbeta": c1 = 7, c2 = 0.75, lower = 0.01, upper = 0.99. "hc5" "hc5m", k, k1, k2, k3 must nonnegative, gamma1 gamma2 must positive. "hcbeta", c1 must nonnegative, c2 must positive, lower upper must lie (0, 1) lower < upper. HCbeta truncation \(w_t = max(lower, min(1 - h_t, upper))\).

title: Heteroskedasticity-consistent covariance estimator — vcov_hc
text: White, H. (1980). heteroskedasticity-consistent covariance matrix estimator direct test heteroskedasticity. Econometrica, 48(4), 817-838. Cribari-Neto, F. (2004). Asymptotic inference heteroskedasticity unknown form. Computational Statistics Data Analysis, 45(2), 215-233. Cribari-Neto, F. da Silva, W. B. (2011). new heteroskedasticity consistent covariance matrix estimator linear regression model. AStA Advances Statistical Analysis, 95(2), 129-146.

title: hcinfer 0.0.0.9000
text: Added initial development version HC covariance estimators, normal Wald inference, S3 output, PublicSchools dataset. plot() now supports vcov_hc() objects, producing leverage-versus-adjustment-factor graphics inspecting relationship h_t g_t. summary() now prints formal test results, confidence interval checks, optional emoji markers improve interpretation robust inference output. Added tests() formal extractor coefficient-level Wald test results. function mirrors API confint(): optional parm argument selects coefficients name position, optional alpha argument recomputes reject column without affecting stored p-values test statistics.
